label: Hrabove — Primary Crash Site
sub: Primary impact zone where the main fuselage of MH17 fell after breaking apart at 33,000 feet. Most victims and structural wreckage recovered here.

label: Torez/Chistyakove — Southern Debris
sub: Southern debris field where sections of MH17 wreckage were recovered, including parts of the fuselage and cargo hold.

label: Rozsypne — Western Debris Field
sub: Western debris scatter zone. Passenger luggage, personal effects, and structural aircraft fragments were discovered across farmland in this area.

label: Petropavlivka — Forward Fuselage Section
sub: Location where the cockpit and forward fuselage section came to rest. The cockpit sustained the direct impact of the 9N314M warhead detonation.

label: Pervomaysk — Buk Launch Field
sub: JIT-confirmed Buk missile launch site: a field approximately 3km south of Snizhne, near the settlement of Pervomaysk. The 9M38 missile was fired from this location at 13:20 UTC on July 17, 2014.

label: Snizhne — Buk Transit City
sub: The Buk TELAR passed through Snizhne en route to the launch field. Citizens photographed and filmed the Buk convoy traveling through the town, providing key OSINT evidence.

label: Donetsk — Buk Convoy Photographed
sub: The Buk TELAR was extensively photographed and filmed passing through Donetsk city on the morning of July 17, 2014. These photos became core OSINT evidence linking the convoy to the 53rd Brigade.

label: Luhansk — Buk Return Route
sub: After the MH17 shootdown, the Buk TELAR (now missing one missile) was photographed in Luhansk on its return journey to Russia. The missing missile is visible in comparison photos.

label: Kursk — 53rd Brigade HQ (Russia)
sub: Home base of Russia's 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade, confirmed by JIT as the source of the Buk TELAR used to shoot down MH17. The brigade's deployment of a Buk to eastern Ukraine was documented by JIT and Bellingcat.

label: Izvaryne — Russia-Ukraine Border Crossing
sub: Russia-Ukraine border crossing point in Luhansk Oblast where the Buk TELAR transited between Russia and Ukraine. JIT documented the Buk's border crossing as part of the confirmed convoy route.

label: Amsterdam Schiphol — Departure Airport
sub: Amsterdam Airport Schiphol, departure point of MH17. The flight departed at 10:15 UTC on July 17, 2014. Schiphol is also the location of the Judicial Complex (Justitieel Complex Schiphol) where the 2020–2022 MH17 criminal trial was held.

label: Gilze-Rijen — JIT Wreckage Reconstruction
sub: Gilze-Rijen Air Base (Vliegbasis Gilze-Rijen) in North Brabant, Netherlands. JIT used the base to transport and reconstruct recovered MH17 wreckage fragments into a partial reconstruction of the aircraft, used as evidence at trial.

label: Kyiv — Ukrainian Government Coordination
sub: Ukrainian capital, home of the Ukrainian member of the JIT. Ukraine contributed intelligence, intercepted communications, and military records to the joint investigation. Ukraine's inclusion in the JIT was critical despite its direct party status in the conflict.

label: Hilversum — JIT Press Conferences
sub: Dutch town in North Holland where several key JIT press conferences were held, broadcast internationally. The Netherlands Forensic Institute (NFI) conducted victim identification and forensic analysis from its laboratory facilities.

label: Strasbourg — European Court of Human Rights
sub: The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) in Strasbourg, France. The Netherlands filed an inter-state application against Russia over MH17. Victim families also filed individual ECHR applications seeking accountability.

label: The Hague — Dutch Prosecution & DSB
sub: The Hague (Den Haag), seat of Dutch law and government. The Dutch Public Prosecution Service (Openbaar Ministerie) and Dutch Safety Board (DSB/Onderzoeksraad) are headquartered here. The Netherlands District Court that issued the MH17 verdict is based here.

label: Schiphol Judicial Complex — Trial Venue
sub: The specially secured Justitieel Complex Schiphol near Amsterdam Airport, where the MH17 murder trial was held from March 2020 to November 2022. The high-security facility was chosen for its capacity to handle a trial of this scale.

label: Vijfhuizen — Nationaal Monument MH17
sub: The Nationaal Monument MH17 at Vijfhuizen (Haarlemmermeer), near Amsterdam. The permanent memorial to all 298 victims features sunflowers and individual memorial elements. Annual July 17 commemorations are held here.

label: Eindhoven Airport — Repatriation Ceremonies
sub: Eindhoven Air Base where the first repatriation flights carrying MH17 victims arrived on July 23, 2014. Dutch King Willem-Alexander, Queen Máxima, and PM Mark Rutte attended the solemn ceremony. Subsequent repatriation flights followed over many months.

label: Hrabove — Crash Site Memorial
sub: Memorial established at the primary crash site near Hrabove village. Sunflowers — many planted by Dutch visitors — have become a symbol of remembrance. Annual gatherings are held by victim families, though the site's location in Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine complicates access.
